FORT CAMPBELL, Ky. Tuesday, hundreds soldiers returned home from nine-month deployment Afghanistan.
"It's excitement (and) nervousness because you never know how the children are going react, but overall just lot excitement," said Lindsay Dunne.
Lindsay Dunne, and her two boys were waiting for their soldier get off the plane and march into the hangar, "The boys were yelling and screaming and was looking for him."
Gail Mintz was there find her little cousin Keandre Corizine, "It feels amazing. are just glad they all made back safely."

After having look this couple times now, figured I'd share approach. While you currently can't technically style previous sibling elements, I've figured out pretty handy workaround.
Suppose you have progress bar with steps that you want style. You want the active step highlighted and all the steps before well (to denote that those steps are completed). Prettying the active step easy, but how you handle the previous steps? You actually style the previous steps styling the following steps.
Let show you what mean....
First, let's get our step navigation built and styled.
The HTML simply unordered list with five list items the first which has "active" class. The line between them actually one solid line that goes behind all the steps, added using the CSS ":before" pseudo-element and positioning place.
Now that the HTML and basic CSS are place, can think about how style the active and completed steps.
You could change the active class these steps many different ways, but I'm going keep simple for this example.
When you click one the steps, the "active" class removed from all them and then re-added the one that was clicked.
Again, you could change this class position many, many different ways, but I'm trying keep simple.
Here's the real secret this whole process: you actually need style the steps following the "active" step, not the previous ones.
Using the "~" sibling selector, can style all the elements after the "active" element. All need style ALL the elements though they're active, and then back and style all the elements after the "active" one though they are the normal state.
This exactly what did (although bit more complex) with CSS-only star rating pen.
